cards.front: Compare WEP, WPA, WPA2, WPA3 — why was each replaced?
cards.back: WEP: RC4 + 40/104-bit key — broken in 2001, trivial to crack. WPA (interim): TKIP, still weak. WPA2: AES-CCMP, 802.11i, secure for years. WPA3: AES-GCMP, SAE handshake (replaces 4-way), forward secrecy, mandatory PMF. Use WPA3 if available; WPA2 minimum.

Personal: shared passphrase used by all clients (PSK). Easy but no per-user identity. Enterprise: each user authenticates via 802.1X to a RADIUS server; unique credentials, can revoke individual users, supports cert-based auth (EAP-TLS). Used in corporate environments.

cards.front: What is the 4-way handshake in WPA2, and what does it derive?
cards.back: Mutual authentication and key derivation between AP and client. Both already share a PMK (Pairwise Master Key — derived from PSK or 802.1X). The 4 messages exchange nonces and confirm message integrity, deriving a fresh PTK (Pairwise Transient Key) used to encrypt the session.

cards.front: What is PMF (Protected Management Frames), and why does WPA3 require it?
cards.back: Encrypts/authenticates 802.11 management frames (deauth, disassociate). Without PMF, attackers can spoof deauth frames to kick clients off, then capture the 4-way handshake or run other attacks. WPA3 mandates PMF; WPA2 supports it as optional.

cards.front: What replaces the PSK 4-way handshake in WPA3-Personal?
cards.back: SAE (Simultaneous Authentication of Equals) — Dragonfly handshake. Resistant to offline dictionary attacks (each guess requires a fresh AP interaction). Provides forward secrecy — even if PSK is compromised later, past sessions remain encrypted.

cards.front: What are EAP-TLS, PEAP, and EAP-FAST?
cards.back: All are 802.1X EAP methods used in WPA2/3-Enterprise. EAP-TLS: mutual cert auth (most secure, requires PKI). PEAP: server cert + inner method (often MSCHAPv2 with username/password) — most common. EAP-FAST: Cisco proprietary, uses PAC instead of certs.
